Question 1 Response: Mechanical ventilator management with reinforcement learning
Question 6 Response:
Summary of Efforts Final Project
CS 238
12/07/23
For this project, we all met up multiple times and worked together to formulate MDP states, actions, and rewards in a ventilator setting, work on the code and discuss which approaches were most efficient, and create the final presentation. We go into more precise detail in each person’s section.

Alyssa Unell: Alyssa helped to formulate the idea of using course concepts to estimate ventilator settings. She also worked on the code base, discussing the implementation of SARSA vs Q-learning and the impact of our reward functions on our results. She assisted in literature review to fully understand the landscape of which the problem was situated in order to create a heuristic for the reward function that could lead to improved policies. She helped with the data processing efforts and has discussions regarding the pros and cons of different discretization efforts for the continuous data. 


Ruhi Sayana: Ruhi helped formulate the MDP setup in the ventilator setting, worked on the code base (parts of data pre-processing and SARSA implementation), contributed to the literature review, and worked on slides in the final presentation. 



Riya Dulepet: Riya worked on helping formulate the MDP setup, worked on the Q-Learning implementation, and reward function ideation. She also assisted in acquiring the dataset and worked on slides in the final presentation.

Question 1 Response: Winning Draughts Championship: Checkers Agent
Question 6 Response: Overall we divided up the work pretty evenly. The coding in this project was the majority of the work, and all 3 of us (Abe, Anthony, Chase) split this responsibility pretty evenly. Anthony contributed majorly on the slides and created pretty much all of the visuals for it. As far as coding, Anthony was very helpful in the Q learning portion of the project, specifically on researching how we could effectively store the states and actions in data structures. This also came into play for monte carlo tree search since for that we had to store the estimated values of nodes in the tree. Abe was really helpful in creating the slides also, and wrote the script for pretty much all of us. As far as coding, he worked pretty much equally on the initial q learning implementations and MCTS portions. It was also him that recommended that we switch fully to MCTS after doing some research and finding that either eval(s) functions used in q learning weren’t always accurate. Abe also coded the board class for both implementations which was certainly non trivial. Finally, Chase was most helpful in coding up the Monte Carlo portion of the project. Chase did research on how Monte Carlo search worked and what hyperparameters such as UCB to use, and with the help of referencing some githubs was able to code out a working Monte Carlo tree search implementation for both of bot.

Question 6 Response:
Samy wrote the baseline probability calculator and significant portions of the Monte Carlo Tree Search solver including the transition, reward, and observation function approximations. Additionally, Samy contributed to the initial project description writeup by doing background research and seeing what previous studies had been conducted in this space.

Luna wrote an alternative solver based on mathematical probability baseline model alternative QMDP solver, and Q-learning reward function, which is wasn’t adopted at the end due to the consideration of computational efficiency. She also had partial contribution to the Monte Carlo Tree Search solver. 

Nikhil wrote the baseline model's policy based on the action-probability linkage described in the presentation. He also contributed towards writing the reward function balancing between losses and wins. Nikhil also wrote an alternative solver based on Neural Network as a value function approximation, but was dropped from the presentation due to time constraints. Nikhil also drafted the initial slides for the presentation.

WordleFS: Wordle Strategies with Forward Search
Question 6 Response:
Andrew worked heavily on the implementation of the Forward Search and Baseline agents. He was in charge of structuring Wordle as an MDP and shaping it to fit the use of Forward Search.  He also worked extensively on quality of life updates to our code (i.e argument parsing, printing out the game state, and logging agent statistics) .

Carlo worked primarily on the implementation of the Wordle clone for the project. Initially implemented as a OpenAI Gym environment for the use of training via Pytorch's DDQN, the game was implemented as an MDP from the start using a step function to update our WordleState each turn. 

We worked together for the rest features not listed above. This mainly includes the finalization of Forward Search through finalizing our Wordle simulation in lookahead. Afterwards, the paper was evenly split among the two of us.

An Exploration of Trends Among Various Methods of Solving 2048
Question 6 Response:
Project 3 Project Contributions
Alex Zhang - alxzhang 
Anna Hudson - afhudson 
CS238
Fall 2023
Professor Kochenderfer

We believe that both team members contributed a roughly equal amount, and we attest that both partners’ efforts were invaluable to the development of the project and presentation. As mentioned previously, our team would like to solve the problem of maximizing a player’s score in 2048. 

Anna Hudson contributions
- manually tested 2048 implementation to ensure game mechanics are implemented properly
- added additional functionality to said implementation
- implemented function to test whether the game is over and 
- implemented function to test whether an attempt to move in some direction is valid
- implemented function to be able to manipulate any arbitrary board tile
- detecting the coordinate position of empty tiles
- together, decided on which 5 algorithms to test to solve 2048
- together, decided on preliminary set of 22 metrics we want to evaluate 
- implemented and debugged 1) Anna Search, 2) Sparse Search (unused), 3) Random Search
- aggregated performance data in spreadsheet and performed analysis
- together, completed slides
- together, completed video recording

Alex Zhang contributions 
- found a julia implementation of 2048 online
- together, decided on which 5 algorithms to test to solve 2048
- together, decided on preliminary set of 22 metrics we want to evaluate 
- implemented and debugged 1) Forward Search, 2) MCTS
- implemented testing harness
- implemented telemetry harnessing system
- ran all algorithms and generated all raw performance data
- created visuals for performance trends 
- together, completed slides
- together, completed video recording
